questions.text: I feel most alive when experiencing something new or intense.
I start new projects or hobbies excited but often abandon them before finishing.
I'm drawn to experiences that are edgy, adventurous, or push my boundaries.
When my work or life becomes too predictable, I feel restless.
I find comfort and satisfaction in familiar routines and established patterns.
When stressed by work or relationships, I reach for food, entertainment, or other pleasures.
I sometimes overspend or overindulge (eating, drinking, shopping) even when I know I should stop.
I think carefully about how I spend money and time on comfort and leisure.
I dive fully into new projects or opportunities when I get excited about them.
I'm more energized by intense short projects than by sustained long-term effort.
Boredom is one of the hardest things for me to tolerate.
Routine and predictability in work or life wear me down over time.
I notice and appreciate beauty, aesthetics, and sensory details more than most people.
I'm drawn toward career or personal activities that carry some risk or uncertainty.
I see humor in things and use it to navigate work and life challenges.
I can laugh at difficult situations without making others feel diminished.
People would say I'm naturally lighthearted and fun to work or socialize with.
Humor helps me process hard things without avoiding the real issue.
I'm aware of how colleagues and social circles perceive me and it affects how I present myself.
I feel a genuine lift when I receive recognition from colleagues or people in my field.
When I accomplish something at work or personally, I want the right people to know about it.
My sense of how I'm doing in my career is affected by feedback from people I respect.
I want acknowledgment from colleagues when I've contributed something meaningful to a project.
When my work goes unrecognized by people I respect professionally, it bothers me.
When someone I'm close to seems disappointed in me, I replay it and look for what I did wrong.
I say yes to people I care about even when the honest answer should be no.
I measure my career progress against specific peers I pay attention to.
When someone in my professional circle succeeds, I notice my internal response before genuine happiness.
I tell people what I honestly see even if it might damage the relationship.
I speak up at work even when it creates friction with people whose opinion matters.
I feel energized by being part of a team, organization, or community.
I invest in the health and cohesion of teams or groups I belong to, even when it costs me.
When I feel rejected by someone professional or personally important, it affects me for days.
When I sense a colleague or person I care about pulling away, I work to re-establish connection.
I feel a pull toward productivity and advancement even during periods meant for rest or vacation.
I take genuine satisfaction in completing projects and reaching professional or personal milestones.
When I'm not making progress toward something meaningful, I feel dissatisfied.
I think about what I'm building in my career that will still matter years from now.
I'm motivated to invest in people and projects that will have impact beyond my direct involvement.
I measure my career success by lasting effect and influence, not just immediate results.
I'm drawn to challenges and opportunities significant enough to matter at a scale beyond my immediate work.
You led a significant project to success. Your team gets credit; your contribution isn't specifically named. Your honest feeling:

options.text: The project succeeded — that's what matters.
Frustration that affects how I feel about the success.

questions.text: You invested significantly in developing a colleague. They go on to success without mentioning your role. Your response:

options.text: Pride — this is exactly what the investment was for.
A sense of loss I carry longer than I think is warranted.
Significant hurt — feels like a failure on their part.

questions.text: You led a significant initiative that failed publicly. You were clearly responsible. In the weeks after:

options.text: I own it clearly and focus forward on what I learned.
I own it publicly but privately revisit what others could have done.
I own my decisions but the public failure is much harder to carry.
I struggle to separate the failure from my own competence.

questions.text: Someone less experienced is promoted into a leadership role above you. You believe it's the wrong choice. Over the following months:

options.text: I commit to making them successful.
I do my job well while privately holding my assessment.
My motivation is genuinely affected, though I manage it.
I look for an exit — I can't sustain full contribution in this structure.

questions.text: I track how my career progress compares to peers at similar stages.
The success of people in my field or company spurs me to push harder.
I'm energized by work environments where performance is ranked or compared.
I feel most effective and like myself when I'm setting direction for a team or project.
I'm drawn to roles where outcomes depend significantly on my ability to influence people.
It's difficult for me to contribute fully without being in a position to shape direction.
I struggle to release work I know could be better, even if further improvement won't be visible.
I'd rather develop genuine expertise in a few areas than be competent across many.
I hold myself to a professional standard that most people would consider unnecessarily high.
